sentences.text: My name is Eva Geiringer Schloss.
Schloss, S-C-H-L-O-S-S.
And Geiringer, G-I-E-R-I-N-G-R.
No.
Geiringer is my maiden name.
I was born in Vienna.
V-I-E-N-N-A.
In Austria.
My-- my father's name was Erich.
Erich Geiringer.
11th of November, 1905.
First memory, I always saw him as a very loving and strong person who always protected me.
Fritzie, short for Elfriede Markovits.
13th of February, 1905.
She was very beautiful, and I looked up to her very much.
And-- but in Vienna, I remember she was-- seemed to be always going out.
And we were always left.
I remember that.
And later, she was much more available to me than at first.
Well, I had a brother who was three years older than me.
And we got on extremely well.
He was more the-- a kind boy, softer, and I was a tomboy.
But as I was three years younger, that fitted well together.
We played always a lot together.
And the first memory when we were about four years old-- when I was four years old.
He was seven.
We had a big house with a big garden, and we had a funny little wagon where he-- where he pulled me.
And we had a drive which went steep down, and I always wanted to have a quick ride in it.
And he didn't.
But that's one of the games we did.
And then there was a big garage with a high wall, and I always wanted to jump down, and-- which I did.
And my brother wouldn't do anything like that at all.
So he admired me, and I admired him.
And he always used to tell me lots of stories.
And one story was he boasted that he could make me cry whenever he wanted to.
And we demonstrated that when my parents had a party, or a few people.
We said we were going to come in and he was going to show that after a few minutes, I was going to burst out into tears.
And what I had to imagine was that we were playing, and he was going to die.
And then I was so upset about it that I burst into tears.
So we came in and he looked at me, and I had to imagine it, that he was going to die.
And then after a few minutes, I really burst into tears, and I just couldn't stop.
And nobody knew how he managed to do this.
But-- so we were always a team.
And we slept together.
And he had a-- a torch which had white, red, and green light.
You had to filter over it.
And at night, he used to tell me ghost stories, and stories about in-- red Indians, and-- and we saw slides shining on the ceiling.
And he made designs with his fingers.
He was very artistic.
And you know, we were a real very happy family.
And then my grandparents-- my mother's parents-- lived in walking distance from where we were, and practically every second day we saw each other.
And I went to sleep there a lot.
And then we had-- my mother had a sister, and my father had a sister.
And they both had children.
So those were our cousins.
My cousin from my father's sister, she was exactly my age, Gaby.
And we were great friends as well.
And so we always went to play together.
And my birthday was in May, and weather was good in May always in Austria, in Vienna.
And we had always a party in the garden.
And we went a lot out on hikes and picnics and swimming.
In winter, skiing.
We were a very sporty family.
Well, my father was.
My mother and my brother less so.
But my father insisted that they come along and do it with us.
So once we went on an excursion.
The Austrian mountains were very near.
And we climbed up mountains, and we came to-- not a rope ladder.
A metal ladder across a precipice.
And my father went ahead, and then we had to follow.
And I right away went after him, and my brother and mother wouldn't go.
So we just walked on on our own.
So I had a wonderful relationship with my father.
And when I was about four years old, he threw me in the swimming pool.
He caught me.
But he said, that's a way you learn to swim.
And I did.
You know, I-- he could do anything with me.
[LAUGHS] And he tried to toughen up my brother, but as I said, he was rather timid.
And we had a big wardrobe in our room, and he put my brother up there, and he said he had to jump down.
And he wouldn't do it.
And my mother stood behind crying and screaming, Erich, don't let him jump.
Don't let him jump.
And my brother sat there, and he didn't dare to jump.
But I said, come on.
Come on.
[LAUGHS] So you know, it was-- it was fun.
Heinz.
My grandmother was called Helena, Helen.
And my grandfather Rudolf.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My aunt, my-- sister of my mother, she got a son about 10 years after.
So we were really the-- her-- their first grandchildren.
So you know, they were very-- we were very spoiled by them, and as I say, we saw them regularly.
And we went, of course, then to school.
Oh, no.
We-- we first lived in this big house in Vienna.
But in about 1934, there was a depression.
And my father had a shoe factory which he inherited from his father, who died rather young.
And so already as a young man he already worked there first.
Later, he become the owner and the manager.
But first, the cousin run it till my father was old enough.
And in the depression, people didn't pay him, and so he couldn't pay his debtor.
And the firm went bankrupt.
And we were told about that, but I don't remember, of course, what arrangement or anything like that.
But I do remember that one day my father came home with a car load of shoes, and we had to sort them out into pairs.
So our whole hall was full of those shoes, and we sorted them.
And I think those were remnants which he still tried to sell off.
And then we had to move to a smaller house in another area.
But it was still nice.
Of-- of Vienna.
And it was more difficult.
I know we-- we didn't get this-- we had more economical food and things like that.
I did notice.
And--
I think that must have been '34, '35 perhaps.
And things were definitely a bit tough for us at the time.
But my father was a very clever businessman, and he pulled the business together again, and so it was all right after a little while.
My father had business connections.
He exported as well.
So that's how he had connections with shoe factories in Holland.

That's how later he could transfer-- well, he transferred money, actually, when he-- then '36 in Germany one knew already that Jews were persecuted, and he was-- well, I thought he was a very clever man.
He realized what might have been.
And he sold shoes to Holland, but he didn't let all the money come back into Austria.
And so he accumulated some capital in Holland, which would help him later to set up business there again.
I, of course, didn't know all of this at the time, but later he told me all that.
Not very much.
I was a bad pupil.
I didn't want to read.
I wasn't interested in reading at all for a long time.
And I had a friend called Liesel.
And she read already.
When we walked, she read all the street signs and the shop signs and everything she could see, and I didn't.
And my mother always pushed me.
And she say, read this.
Read this.
And I-- I wasn't been interested.
Perhaps I couldn't even.
But I didn't do it.
And-- but I was very playful.
I always wanted to play.
And was very un-serious, really.
And what I do remember is there were not many Jewish children in the school.
And after school, the Jewish children had to go out of the class for religious instructions.
So-- but that was before Hitler.
Had So it was already marked who was Jewish and who wasn't.
And--
The children weren't very interested.
I did learn the Hebrew alphabet and a little bit of Bible stories.
But nobody really liked it, and we always irritated the teachers.
So it wasn't very disciplined.
But we had to go.
That was definite.
No choice.
And I didn't mind going to school, but I certainly didn't like it.
And my brother was a very keen student.
He was very good, and he loved going to school.
So that was in the beginning.
Later on, luckily I became better.
[LAUGHS]
There, we-- my mother had friends called the Hahns, and they had a little boy called Martin.
And he was a very-- he was two years younger than me.
And I liked him very, very much.
And we played practically every day, first before we went to school and then after school, always went there with my mother and his mother.
And recently, we found him, actually.
After 50 years.
We-- he-- or his wife read a article, magazine article in Australia.
They went to Australia.
About-- a review about my book.
And so she tried to get in touch, and she found me.
And only now they came to England, and they got in touch.
And I met them again.
And his wife was actually the daughter of my father's best friend.
And I remember her as well.
But I mean, they didn't live so near.
So I didn't see them that much.
But her father, I remember very well, because my father often talked about him.
He was called Fritz Mendel.
He died a few years ago in-- they went back to Vienna.
He died there.
Otherwise, we have actually no connection at all with anybody or-- in Austria.
Nearly all our ties were completely broken.
My mother kept touch with a few friends who immigrated to South America.
But after years, you know, the correspondence stopped, of course, because the war broke out and you couldn't write.
And after the war, we didn't really have any contact.
They lived already in that other district, so they were near.
Till we left, till 19-- about August, 1938.
That's when we immigrated.
No.
In the-- in the end, after the Nazis had come to Austria, we were not allowed to go to ordinary schools.
We had to go to Jewish schools.
I can't remember anything about that.
But it very-- it was a very short period, perhaps three, four weeks.
So I only know that it was far to go by train.
But the school itself, I can't recollect at all.
Our family was very tense in the last year or so, because obviously it was obvious that something was going to happen.
And-- but we didn't really-- I mean, I certainly didn't quite understand what was going on.
And one day, we-- on the radio, we-- we-- my parents put the radio on, and we heard that the Austrians had let the Nazis, Germans come in voluntarily.
And so later in the morning, we all watched from the windows when the armies came in.
And the Austrians stood in the road waving flags and shouting, and they were really received as heroes, and they were very delighted that the Germans had come in.
And all the Jewish population were, of course, in shock.
And we were a very assimilated family.
So we had a lot of Jewish friends.
We never kind of told people we were Jewish.
And we had a lot of non-Jewish friends.
And as soon as the Germans entered, those non-Jewish friends started to not to want to see us, not to want to talk to us in the street, and really avoiding us.
And that was really the first time I realized that Jewish-- to be Jewish was something which-- I was something which you shouldn't be.
And I asked my father what-- why?
I mean, I'm just the same as some of my other friends.
And I just couldn't understand.
And my father couldn't really explain to me why it was supposed to be bad.
And he said, we just have to try to make the most of it.
But we'll try to get out as soon as possible.
We went occasionally to the synagogue.
There was a synagogue in walking distance, very near us, a reform synagogue.
And on high holy days, we went there.
But it-- it can't actually have been that reform, because I remember on the-- Yom Kippur, and so people took off their shoes and-- and slippers.
And that I remember.
I thought it was very funny.
So I don't know if perhaps the reformed Jews were more orthodox than they are here now.
But this is something which impressed me.
But otherwise, I have very little recollection of synagogue or-- I remember we did go occasionally.
But that was really all I remember of being Jewish.
My brother then, actually, he joined a Jewish youth movement.
But I wouldn't know what it was.
And it was sort of like Boy Scouts, but a Jewish movement.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think it was Zionist, but our family was not Zionist.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
I think that is all the recollection of our Jewishness.
Well, it changed very rapidly because, as I said, we had to go change schools.
And my brother, who did look rather Jewish-- he had a big nose and dark hair-- and he got attacked at school from his friends.
And he got beaten up.
And the teachers just watched, and didn't help him.
And as I said to you, he was a very timid boy.
And he was terribly upset.
And when he came home bleeding, my father said, well, we have to see that he gets out of the country as soon as possible.
So my father left really first, and then my brother.
And my mother and me stayed behind.
We were trying to sell the furniture and some belongings so that we would have some more money.
But of course, the Christians knew that we had to sell.
So they gave ridiculous prices.
So it was really not worthwhile to stay behind for the little bit of things we could sell.
So my brother went to join my father in Holland.
And my mother and me went a little bit later, few months later.
But we didn't get a permit to go to Holland, and we had actually-- we had an exit permit, but no permit to go anywhere.
So we went to Belgium.
But on-- on the way, we had to sort of lose our papers and enter Belgium illegally as refugees.
How did you travel?
By train.
And then part of the-- between borders, we walked.
Of course, with hardly any luggage.
And--
Just a little suitcase, very little.
And we went to Brussels.
And I mean, we had no idea where to go or what to do.
And there was a-- a committee of-- I think from the American joined or-- Jewish people where we had to register.
And they put us up somewhere in a room at first.
And it was everything in French, of course.
I didn't-- my mother did speak French, but I didn't understand or speak anything like that.

And then my father, who lived not in Amsterdam but in the south of Holland, and he said to us, well, it doesn't really matter if you live in Amsterdam or Brussels, because for me it would be just the same distance to travel.
And he was coming then most weekends to visit.
So once my father came, he brought money.
And he found us a boarding house.
We had one room there with my brother and my mother and me.
And when my father came weekend, we hired another little room where my mother and my father could stay.
So it was very primitive, actually.
And I had to go straightaway to a French school, which-- as I said, I didn't like school anyway.
And this was of course horrendous because they were not use to-- there were very few refugees at this time.
And they had no instructions what to do with refugee children, to teach us.
So they just didn't really take any notice.
They just spoke to me in French and gave me a seat, and that was it.
And I couldn't follow anything.
I didn't know what it-- what was happening.
And after a few weeks, there was a dictation.
And I had to write down-- and French, of course, is a difficult language to write anyway.
And whatever I wrote, every word was wrong, of course.

And the next day when they got the works back, with the mistakes underlined in red, the teacher showed my work to the class with everything, of course, in red, because there was not a single word correct, to show how stupid I was.
When I think about this now, how people could do things like that.
It's just unbelievable.
They had no idea yet how to treat children or how to help refugees, or-- it was terrible.
So luckily, this teacher moved then to another class or left school, and there came a different teacher.
A very nice woman.
And she was very kind to me, and she helped me a little bit over this very difficult period.
And then it was my birthday.
And I spoke already French, and it was actually then I liked it very much.
It was all very orderly.
And I enjoyed school.
And the Belgium girls were-- it was just a girls' school-- were very, very nice.
And I ask in the boarding house, and asked my mother if I could have a little birthday party.
And I was allowed a few girls.
And so there were two particular girls in the class whom I liked very much.
And I asked them to come to my birthday party.
And they said yes, of course they'd come.
And the next day, they brought a note from their parents that they were not allowed to come.
And I went home crying, and I asked my mother why-- why can't they come?
My mother said, well, you are a refugee.
You are Jewish.
And those people don't know.
You don't live in your own apartment.
You live in a boarding house.
And they don't want to mix with us.
So that was quite a shock to me.
But I still had a party.
I had a few non-- a few Jewish friends, refugees, who came.
One of those girls was called Kitty.
She came from Vienna as well.
She was a friend of my parents, the parents of her.
And she lived as well nearby.
And my brother liked her very much.
And she was a very lively girl.
And she came.
And then also a few Jewish children.
This family of Kitty, they all were deported, and nobody returned of that family.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so my father came to visit.
And it was then after a while, we quite got used to it and we quite liked it.
And the boarding house owners had a little boy living there called Jackie, and I played a lot with him.
And he taught me a lot of French.
And I was quite happy there.
There was another refugee family living.
They were called [?
Deutsch, ?] from Germany.
And they were an older couple.
And they had no children.
And they sort of adopted me.
And they were quite well-to-do, and they always gave me presents and chocolates.
And so I was quite spoiled in this boarding house.
I quite enjoyed it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But my father was a little bit uneasy that we would live in Belgium and he in Holland.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] yes, in 1939, the war started.
And-- with Germany and England.
And he said it's really a bit dangerous.
And so he applied for a permit for us to visit in Holland, to visit him, so to say.
And we did get a visiting permit.
And so we went-- in February, 1940, we went to Amsterdam.
